title: C++ Variables
content:
### Variables in C++

Variables are containers for storing data values. Every variable must have a declared type before use.

---

### Declaring Variables

```cpp
int age;             // Declaration
int age = 25;        // Declaration + initialization
int x, y, z;         // Multiple declarations
auto value = 42;     // Type deduction (C++11)
```

---

### Variable Naming Rules

| Rule | Valid Examples | Invalid Examples |
|------|---------------|-----------------|
| Letters, digits, underscore | `myVar`, `_count`, `value2` | `2value` (starts with digit) |
| Case-sensitive | `Age` vs `age` | `my-var` (hyphen not allowed) |
| Not keywords | `myInt` | `int` (reserved keyword) |

---

### Initialization Styles

```cpp
int a = 5;       // Copy initialization
int b(5);        // Direct initialization
int c{5};        // Brace initialization (C++11) - preferred
int d = {5};     // Copy brace initialization
```

---

### Scope and Lifetime

| Scope | Keyword | Lifetime |
|-------|---------|----------|
| Local | None | Block scope |
| Global | None | Program lifetime |
| Static | `static` | Program lifetime, file scope |
| External | `extern` | Across files |

---

> **Key Point:** Always initialize variables before using them. Uninitialized variables contain garbage values and lead to undefined behavior.
